title: Nigeria Trade Policy Update — Q1 2026 Import Regulation Changes
summary: Summary of key Nigeria import regulation changes effective Q1 2026. NCS enforcement of 15-year vehicle age limit tightened. SON certification now mandatory before vessel departure (not on arrival). New NICIS-2 mandatory fields for vehicle imports.
body: Nigeria Trade Policy Update — Q1 2026 Import Regulation Changes. Nigeria Trade Desk summarises key regulatory changes affecting vehicle importers on the US–Nigeria corridor. CHANGE 1 — SON PRE-DEPARTURE CERTIFICATION: Effective 1 February 2026, the Standards Organisation of Nigeria (SON) has tightened its certification rules. SON certification must now be obtained before the vessel departs the load port. Previously, SON certification on arrival was accepted in some cases. This change eliminates the 'fix it on arrival' approach. Practical impact: importers must allow 5–10 days for SON certification before cargo receipt at Baltimore or Savannah. CHANGE 2 — 15-YEAR AGE LIMIT ENFORCEMENT: Nigeria Customs Service (NCS) has received new guidance to strictly enforce the 15-year vehicle age prohibition. Vehicles manufactured before January 2011 are now prohibited. NCS at Tin Can and Apapa have been instructed to enforce this without waivers. Vehicles found to be in violation: seized and destroyed. No duty refund. CHANGE 3 — NEW NICIS-2 MANDATORY FIELDS: From 1 March 2026, the following additional fields are mandatory in NICIS-2 PAAR submissions for vehicle imports: Vehicle Identification Number (VIN), Year of manufacture, SON certificate number, Fumigation certificate number. Submissions without these fields will be rejected by the system. CHANGE 4 — DUTY PAYMENT WINDOW: NCS has reduced the duty payment window from 30 days to 21 days after PAAR generation. After 21 days, the PAAR expires and a new assessment (potentially at a different FX rate) must be generated. This change came into effect 1 January 2026.

title: Nigeria Business Environment Report — March 2026
summary: Nigeria business environment snapshot for March 2026. Economy growing at 3.2% GDP (NBS Q4 2025). Inflation 28.4%. FX stabilising. Ease of doing business improving marginally. Port reform progressing. Key risks: insecurity in some corridors, power reliability.
body: Nigeria Business Environment Report — March 2026. Nigeria Trade Desk publishes monthly snapshots of the Nigerian business environment relevant to importers and freight forwarders. MACROECONOMIC OVERVIEW: GDP growth: 3.2% year-on-year (Q4 2025, NBS). Real sector growth is concentrated in telecoms, trade, and financial services. Oil sector growth remains constrained by production disruptions. Inflation: 28.4% (February 2026). Gradually declining from the 33%+ peak of mid-2024 but still significantly above the CBN's 21.4% upper band target. Interest rates: CBN MPR at 27.25%, expected to hold at April MPC. Unemployment: Estimated 33% (expanded definition) per NBS 2024 survey. FX STATUS: USD/NGN: 1,601 official / 1,647–1,658 parallel. The premium between official and parallel markets (3–4%) is the tightest it has been since pre-2015, reflecting the success of the CBN FX unification policy. PORT REFORM PROGRESS: The Buhari/Tinubu administrations' port reform agenda is progressing. NPA has contracted a port consultant to review terminal concession agreements. The NPA is also piloting an electronic berth allocation system (see port watch reports). These reforms are positive for importers but will take 12–24 months to materially improve turnaround times. KEY OPERATIONAL RISKS FOR IMPORTERS: (1) Power cuts: 12–18 hours of grid power per day on average in Lagos. Generators required for all business operations. (2) Road infrastructure: Lagos road congestion remains severe, particularly on Apapa access routes. (3) Security: The broader Nigeria security situation (primarily northwest) does not materially affect Lagos port operations.

title: US–Nigeria Trade Corridor — Volume Statistics 2025 and 2026 Outlook
summary: US–Nigeria trade statistics 2025. Total US exports to Nigeria: $4.2bn. Vehicles and automotive parts: $620m (15% of total). Ro-ro vehicle imports via sea: estimated 58,000 units from the US in 2025. Volume growth expected 8–10% in 2026.
body: US–Nigeria Trade Corridor Volume Statistics — 2025 and 2026 Outlook. Nigeria Trade Desk compiles trade statistics for the US–Nigeria corridor from USTR, US Census Bureau, and NBS data. 2025 TRADE OVERVIEW: Total US merchandise exports to Nigeria: $4.2 billion (estimated, 2025 actuals pending final NBS publication). Key export categories: (1) Aircraft, spacecraft and parts: $680m (16%). (2) Vehicles, automotive parts: $620m (15%). (3) Machinery and mechanical appliances: $580m (14%). (4) Petroleum products: $410m (10%). (5) Other manufactured goods: remainder. VEHICLE IMPORTS VIA RO-RO: Estimated US to Nigeria ro-ro vehicle shipments (2025): 58,000 units. Dominant carriers: Sallaum Lines (~55%), Grimaldi Lines (~35%), other/container (~10%). Primary discharge port: Lagos (Tin Can + Apapa) — 82% of US ro-ro imports. Secondary: Onne port, Rivers State — 8%. Remainder: Calabar, Warri, Cotonou (re-export). GROWTH TREND: 2023: 49,000 units from US. 2024: 52,500 units. 2025: 58,000 units. CAGR (2023–2025): 8.8%. 2026 OUTLOOK: Volume growth of 8–10% expected, driven by corporate fleet renewal, government procurement, and improved FX access. The Sallaum bi-weekly service (June 2026) is a direct response to this demand. Estimated 2026 volumes: 62,000–64,000 units from the US. PRICING CONTEXT: Average CIF value of a vehicle imported from the US to Nigeria: $18,000–$22,000. At the current duty rate (35% + 7.5% VAT), total duties on a $20,000 vehicle are approximately ₦11.2m at CBN rate.

title: Checklist for US Vehicle Exporters Shipping to Nigeria — 2026 Edition
summary: Complete checklist for US exporters and importers shipping vehicles to Nigeria in 2026. Covers pre-shipment, customs, and post-arrival requirements. Updated for 2026 SON, NICIS-2 and PAAR changes.
body: US Vehicle Exporter Checklist — Nigeria 2026. Nigeria Trade Desk publishes this checklist for US exporters, Nigerian importers, and freight forwarders involved in US–Nigeria vehicle shipments. PRE-SHIPMENT (at least 3 weeks before vessel departure): ☐ Confirm vehicle year of manufacture (must be 2011 or newer). ☐ Obtain SON (Standards Organisation of Nigeria) certification — allow 5–10 business days. ☐ Obtain APHIS Phytosanitary Certificate from USDA. ☐ Arrange fumigation and obtain fumigation certificate. ☐ Apply for Form M at Nigerian bank — allow 5–10 business days. ☐ Obtain Combined Certificate of Value and Origin (CCVO) from US Chamber of Commerce. ☐ Prepare commercial invoice with CIF value broken down. AT CARGO RECEIPT (before vessel departure): ☐ Deliver vehicle to terminal by cargo receipt deadline. ☐ Ensure vehicle is clean (photographic evidence required). ☐ Confirm export AES filing (automated export system) completed. ☐ Obtain Bill of Lading (B/L) from carrier after vessel departure. AFTER VESSEL DEPARTURE: ☐ File PAAR on NICIS-2 with: Form M number, B/L, invoice, CCVO, SON certificate, VIN, year of manufacture, fumigation certificate. ☐ Pay import duty online via e-payment gateway. ☐ Confirm PAAR number with customs clearing agent. ☐ Engage Port Agent at Lagos (required for all vessel arrivals). ARRIVAL AT LAGOS: ☐ Monitor vessel ETA via carrier and AIS sources. ☐ Confirm berth allocation with NPA (via port agent). ☐ Present PAAR number for NCS examination. ☐ Collect vehicle after NCS release and NPA gate clearance. ☐ Pay terminal handling charges and storage (if applicable).

title: Nigeria Port Reform Scorecard — Q1 2026 Progress Assessment
summary: Nigeria port reform progress scorecard Q1 2026. Electronic berth allocation pilot at Tin Can: on track (April launch). NPA terminal concession review: delayed. Truck booking system rollout: 60% complete. Overall reform momentum: moderate.
body: Nigeria Port Reform Scorecard — Q1 2026 Assessment. Nigeria Trade Desk tracks the progress of the NPA port reform programme against stated objectives. The programme aims to reduce ship turnaround time at Lagos from the current average of 9.2 days to a target of 5 days by 2028. INITIATIVE 1 — Electronic Berth Pre-Allocation (Tin Can Island): Status: ON TRACK. The NPA has confirmed the April 2026 pilot launch at Tin Can Island. The system has been tested with 5 carrier agents. Expected impact: reduce anchor wait by 1–2 days by end of 2026 as the system matures. Nigeria Trade Desk assessment: Promising, but implementation quality during the pilot period (April–June) will determine whether the rollout accelerates. INITIATIVE 2 — Terminal Concession Review: Status: DELAYED. The planned comprehensive review of terminal concession agreements (originally due Q4 2025) has been postponed to Q3 2026. The delay is attributed to complex negotiations with existing concessionaires. Impact: The review is a prerequisite for major terminal investment decisions. Delay pushes back infrastructure improvements by 6–9 months. INITIATIVE 3 — Electronic Truck Management (TMS): Status: IN PROGRESS. The TMS truck booking system is operational at Apapa Quays (70% of trucks using it). Tin Can TMS rollout is 50% complete, target full rollout June 2026. Impact: Reducing peak-hour gate congestion by an estimated 20% at Apapa. INITIATIVE 4 — NIMASA Inspection Digitalisation: Status: EARLY STAGE. NPA and NIMASA are piloting digital pre-arrival inspection dossiers for vessels, which could reduce inspection waiting time from 38 hours to under 12 hours. Full rollout: Q4 2026. OVERALL ASSESSMENT: Moderate progress. The reform agenda is moving, but turnaround time improvement will not be felt materially until H2 2026 at the earliest.
